title: Christianity
summary: The religious movement that began as a Jewish sect in Roman Palestine, became the official religion of the Roman Empire, shaped Western civilization's law, art, philosophy, and political thought for two thousand years, and now claims more than two billion adherents worldwide — the largest and most geographically diverse religion in history.
hook: Christianity began as a rumor in a province of the Roman Empire: that a executed Jew had risen from the dead. Within three centuries, that rumor had become the official religion of the empire that killed him. Within fifteen centuries, it had reshaped every aspect of European civilization. Understanding why is one of the central questions of world history.

beginner.thePeriod:
Around the year 30 CE, Roman soldiers executed a Jewish preacher named Jesus on a wooden cross outside Jerusalem. Crucifixion was a punishment for criminals and rebels. By every normal measure, his movement should have died with him.

Instead his followers began telling people that he had risen from the dead. Small groups who believed this story formed in cities all across the Roman Empire. They shared meals, cared for the sick, and welcomed people that Roman society often ignored.

Within about three hundred years, the same empire that had executed its founder made this once-banned movement its state faith. For most of the two thousand years since, Christianity has been the main religion of Europe. European ships and armies later carried it to the Americas, Africa, and Asia.

As of the 2020s, more than two billion people call themselves Christian. That makes it one of the biggest religions on Earth. Its center has been shifting too, with rapid growth in Africa and Latin America while church attendance falls across much of Europe.
beginner.theBoundaries:
What holds Christianity together is the story of Jesus: his life, his death, and the belief that he rose again. But people have argued about what that story means since the very beginning.

The earliest Christians disagreed about big questions. Was Jesus a human, a divine being, or somehow both? Did believers still have to follow Jewish law? These arguments led to large meetings, written statements of belief, and lasting splits.

Over time the tradition broke into major branches: Catholic, Orthodox, and Protestant, with hundreds of smaller groups inside each one. As Christianity spread, it kept adapting. Sometimes it absorbed local customs. Sometimes European rulers forced it on people they had conquered.
beginner.theConditions:
Christianity grew out of Judaism in a specific place and time: Roman-controlled Palestine. Three forces met there. Jewish tradition gave the new movement its scriptures, its idea of one God, and its sense of right and wrong.

The Roman Empire gave it roads, a shared language, and busy cities. A believer could travel from town to town and find people who could understand the message. Greek thinking gave Christians tools to explain their beliefs in careful arguments.

Why did a small Jewish group turn into a world religion? Scholars still debate this. A few causes stand out. Believers were sure Jesus had risen. They welcomed non-Jews without demanding circumcision. Their communities were well organized. And in 312 CE, the emperor Constantine joined them.
beginner.internalDiversity:
Christianity has always held many forms at once. Even the earliest Christian writings show different ways of understanding Jesus.

For centuries, leaders argued about who Jesus really was. In 325 CE a famous meeting produced a shared statement called the Nicene Creed. In 1054 a major split divided Eastern and Western Christianity. In the 1500s the Protestant Reformation broke Western Christianity into many separate groups.

Because of all this, the word Christianity covers an enormous range. It includes ancient Ethiopian services sung in old languages. It also includes American churches with rock bands and giant screens. It includes movements focused on helping the poor and movements that promise wealth to believers.
beginner.longConsequences:
It is hard to exaggerate how much Christianity shaped the world. In Europe it influenced law, schools, art, philosophy, and how people thought about power. Monasteries kept learning alive. The Church ran courts and trained scholars. Kings and church leaders fought for centuries over who should be in charge.

Through European empires, Christianity reached every continent. It became the main religion of the Americas and large parts of Africa and Asia.

This spread had two faces. Sometimes people chose the faith freely. Sometimes it was forced on them by violence. Missionaries built hospitals and schools, but they also helped destroy local religions and ways of life. That mixed legacy is tied to the history of colonial power and is still argued about.
beginner.periodizationDebate:
Historians ask a tricky question: how should we divide up Christianity's long history?

Is it one continuous religion running from its earliest days to the present? Or were some changes so huge that they almost created a different religion under the same name? People point to three turning points: when Rome adopted the faith, the split of East and West in 1054, and the Reformation. Each one changed how Christians worshipped, who led them, and what they believed mattered most.

This is not just a question about the past. Modern Christianity is so varied that people still argue about what counts as truly Christian. A church in Nigeria, a monastery in Greece, and a megachurch in Texas all claim the same name, yet they can look very different. Sorting out what they share is part of what makes studying this religion so hard.

general.thePeriod:
Around 30 CE, Roman authorities crucified a Jewish preacher from Galilee outside Jerusalem. Crucifixion was reserved for slaves, rebels, and the lowest criminals. A movement led by an executed provincial had almost no reason to survive, let alone spread.

Yet it did both. Within a generation, communities of his followers had appeared in cities across the empire, bound by the claim that he had risen from the dead. They tended to welcome people of low status, included women in unusual numbers, and cared for the sick during epidemics. Within roughly three centuries, the faith that Rome had once persecuted became the religion Rome endorsed.

For most of the next two thousand years, Christianity dominated Europe, then spread worldwide through European expansion. As of the 2020s it counts more than two billion adherents, placing it among the biggest of world religions. Its demographic weight has shifted south, and the number of Christians in Africa alone now rivals the number in Europe.
general.theBoundaries:
Christianity coheres around the death and resurrection of Jesus, but the meaning of those events has been fought over from the start. The forms later branded as heresy were often not fringe distortions. They were serious rival versions of the faith that lost the contest to define it.

The Council of Nicaea in 325 CE shows how this worked. The emperor Constantine called it to settle a dispute about whether Christ was fully equal to God, and it set a lasting precedent: imperial power helping decide religious orthodoxy. The creed it produced satisfied few people completely, and the views it rejected still commanded a following in many regions. Excluded groups such as Arians and Nestorians persisted for centuries outside the official church. The historian Bart Ehrman has argued that the early tradition was a field of competitors, and the winners later wrote the history.
general.theConditions:
The apostle Paul shaped what Christianity became almost as much as Jesus did. His letters, the earliest surviving Christian documents, framed Jesus's death as an atonement for sin, opened the movement to non-Jews without requiring Jewish law, and built a network of house churches across the empire. Paul was a Greek-speaking Jew who could move between Jewish and Greek worlds, which let him define a faith that appealed to both.

The empire's other gifts mattered too: shared roads, a common trade language, and dense city life that let ideas travel. The standard story says Christianity then won converts gradually and peacefully. The historian Ramsay MacMullen has complicated that picture. He points to anti-pagan laws, mob attacks on temples, and pressure from bishops, arguing that coercion played a real part in the religion's triumph. Persuasion and force, it seems, worked together rather than one excluding the other.
general.internalDiversity:
The Reformation of the sixteenth century did more than split Western Christianity. Its longer consequence was the slow rise of religious toleration as a political value. Once Christians were killing one another over doctrine, arguments for letting people worship differently gained force. The wars of religion helped make a more secular politics thinkable.

The sharper change in recent decades is geographic. Christianity's center of gravity has moved south, toward Africa, Latin America, and parts of Asia. Movements there are not simply European faith in local dress. Pentecostalism, with its emphasis on healing and the Holy Spirit, liberation theology, with its focus on the poor, and independent African churches all represent genuine innovations. Because most Christians now live outside Europe and North America, the questions raised in the global South are likely to shape the religion's future more than debates in Western churches.
general.longConsequences:
Christianity's mark on European law runs from the medieval period to the present. Canon law, the law of the Church, ranked among the more sophisticated legal systems of medieval Europe and offered a model that secular courts later drew on. The idea of natural law, meaning moral rules that reason can discover and that bind all people, was worked out within a Christian framework, notably by Thomas Aquinas, and fed into later theories of human rights.

Through European empires, the faith reached every continent and became dominant across the Americas and much of Africa and Asia. That spread carried both sincere conversion and violent imposition, often at the same time. Missions destroyed local traditions while also building hospitals, schools, and relief networks. The result is a legacy hard to separate from colonial power. Present-day arguments over apologies, returned artifacts, and the ties between missions and empire all turn on how that history is judged.
general.periodizationDebate:
How to divide Christianity's history is itself contested, and the stakes are not purely academic. One charged question is its relationship to Judaism, the parent tradition it grew from and then defined itself against. Scholars once pictured a clean early break, but the split now looks slower and messier. Jewish-Christian communities lasted for centuries, and firm boundaries came late. Because Christian hostility to Jews helped pave the way to the Holocaust, this genealogy carries weight that a purely factual account cannot fully hold.

A second question comes from the global South. For two centuries the standard story ran from the Mediterranean through Rome, medieval Europe, the Reformation, and on to modern times, treating the religion's global reach as something recent. But African Christianity in Egypt and Ethiopia, and Asian Christianity in Syria and India, have ancient roots that predate European dominance. A genuinely global account of the religion would not run through Europe at all, and it would be told quite differently.

advanced.thePeriod: The historical Jesus — the Galilean preacher whose life and teachings generated the Christian movement — is known through documents written decades after his death by people who had not met him. The quest for the historical Jesus — which has occupied biblical scholars since the eighteenth century — has established some facts with reasonable confidence: he was a Jewish preacher in Roman Palestine, he attracted followers, he was crucified by Roman authorities, and within a few years his followers were proclaiming his resurrection. Everything beyond this minimal core is contested. John Meier's four-volume A Marginal Jew applies the full toolkit of historical-critical method to establish what can be known. Bart Ehrman's more accessible accounts have brought the scholarly debates to a general audience while maintaining the essential distinction between historical evidence and theological belief.
advanced.theBoundaries:
Bart Ehrman's work on the variety of early Christianity — particularly Lost Christianities — has shown that the forms of Christianity that were declared heretical in the second and third centuries were not marginal deviations from an original orthodoxy but genuine competitors for the Christian tradition. Gnostic Christianity, Marcionite Christianity, Jewish Christianity — these were not perversions of an original Christianity but early developments that lost the competition to define the tradition. The winners wrote history; the losers' texts were suppressed or destroyed. The discovery of the Nag Hammadi library in 1945 — a cache of Gnostic texts buried in the Egyptian desert — provided direct evidence of the traditions the proto-orthodox suppressed.

The relationship between the Council of Nicaea and Constantine's political interests has been debated since the council itself. Constantine did not make Christianity Rome's official religion — that happened under Theodosius in 380 CE — but he did use his authority to resolve theological disputes and to define orthodoxy. Whether the Nicene Creed represents theological truth or political compromise — or both — is a question that Christians have been arguing ever since. The historical account and the theological account of Nicaea's authority are genuinely different questions, though they are frequently conflated.
advanced.theConditions:
Ramsay MacMullen's work on the Christianization of the Roman Empire complicates the standard narrative of gradual voluntary conversion. MacMullen shows that coercion — state enforcement of anti-pagan laws, mob violence against temples, episcopal pressure on local populations — played a significant role in the triumph of Christianity. The Roman Empire did not simply choose Christianity; it was also compelled toward it by a combination of imperial policy, Christian social pressure, and the destruction of alternatives.

The sociology of early Christianity's growth has been analyzed systematically by Rodney Stark, whose The Rise of Christianity (1997) applies sociological models to explain why Christianity grew faster than competing religious movements. Stark's key factors: Christianity's high retention of women (who had higher status in Christian communities than in pagan ones), its effective care for the sick during epidemics (which increased survival rates and gratitude), and its clear boundaries of belonging (which created strong in-group solidarity). Whether these factors sufficiently explain Christianity's growth without reference to its theological content is contested, but Stark's framework shifted the terms of the historical debate.
advanced.internalDiversity:
The theological substance of Southern Christianity, not merely its numerical weight, is what the recent scholarship of "World Christianity" has tried to take seriously. Lamin Sanneh argued that Christianity is unusual among world religions in being endlessly translatable — it has no untranslatable sacred language, so every vernacular Bible re-roots the faith in a new culture and hands ownership to local believers rather than importing a fixed European product; on his account the Southern churches are not derivative but the predictable fruit of a religion that indigenizes wherever it lands. Philip Jenkins, in The Next Christendom, drew the more uncomfortable implication: the Christianity now ascendant across the global South tends to be more supernaturalist, more morally traditional, and more biblically literal than its Northern counterpart, which sets up durable conflict — over sexuality, scriptural authority, and who gets to speak for the tradition — between a shrinking liberal North and a growing conservative South.

The feminist critique of Christian institutions has engaged seriously with both the egalitarian potential of Christian theology (Paul's claim that 'there is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female') and the patriarchal institutions that have characterized most Christian traditions. Feminist biblical scholarship — figures like Elisabeth Schüssler Fiorenza and Phyllis Trible — has recovered the roles of women in early Christianity that were suppressed in canonical texts and by institutional church development. The ordination of women, which divides Roman Catholicism and many evangelical traditions from mainline Protestant denominations, represents the live contemporary version of this historical debate.
advanced.longConsequences:
Diarmaid MacCulloch's A History of Christianity (2009) is the most comprehensive recent synthesis, covering the full two-thousand-year history with attention to non-Western traditions usually marginalized in standard accounts. MacCulloch emphasizes the diversity and contingency of Christian history — the many points at which things could have gone differently — and resists the teleological narrative in which the tradition moves toward its present form. His account of the Eastern churches, of African Christianity, and of Christianity's global spread gives the religion's history a genuinely global rather than Eurocentric frame.

Christianity's imprint on Western law is now argued in far stronger terms than the textbook observation that the Church's courts were sophisticated and that natural law fed later rights theory. Harold Berman's Law and Revolution located the origin of the entire Western legal tradition in the Papal Revolution of the eleventh and twelfth centuries: when the Gregorian reformers asserted the Church's legal autonomy against kings and emperors, they created for the first time competing jurisdictions and a self-conscious, professional body of law — the template from which secular legal systems were later built. A bolder strand presses past law into politics. Carl Schmitt's claim that "all significant concepts of the modern theory of the state are secularized theological concepts" has been taken up by historians such as Brad Gregory, whose The Unintended Reformation traces disenchantment, the autonomous market, and the rights-bearing individual back to late-medieval theological mutations and the Reformation's shattering of a shared moral order. The dispute is ultimately about directionality — whether the modern secular order is Christianity's heir or its repudiation — and it is the same unresolved question that hangs over the natural-law-versus-human-rights debate.
advanced.periodizationDebate:
The relationship between Christianity and the development of science has been contested since the Galileo affair. The older 'conflict' narrative — Christianity systematically suppressing science — has been largely rejected by historians of science. The newer 'complexity' narrative emphasizes that Christianity both provided the institutional framework for early modern science (universities, learned societies) and created the theological problems (the nature of Providence, the reading of scripture) that generated conflicts. The Galileo case was as much a conflict about authority and institutional power as about the content of scientific claims.

The deeper challenge to the standard periodization is not that it begins too late but that it assumes a single center at all. Historians of World Christianity — Andrew Walls foremost among them — argue that the faith has been serially polycentric from the start: its demographic and intellectual heartland has migrated from Jerusalem to the Hellenistic Mediterranean, to Latin Europe, to the North Atlantic, and now toward the global South, with no location permanently central and each shift carrying a near-total transfer of vitality. On this model the European centuries were one phase among several rather than the trunk from which everything else branches, and the old churches of Ethiopia, Syria, and the Malabar coast were never peripheral survivals but evidence that the faith was multicentred before Europe itself was Christian. A genuinely global account would trade the single timeline for several overlapping ones.

foundation.theStory: Christianity began as a Jewish sect and became a world religion through a series of transformations that were neither inevitable nor planned: the inclusion of Gentiles, the conversion of Constantine, the development of a theological tradition that could speak to Greek philosophy, and eventually the spread through European colonialism to every continent. Understanding these transformations is essential for understanding Western civilization and the contemporary world.
foundation.theDebate: The central scholarly debates concern the historical Jesus (what can be known about him?), the origins of Christianity (why did this particular Jewish sect survive and expand while others did not?), the nature of early Christian diversity (was there an original orthodoxy that heresies deviated from, or multiple competing versions?), and the role of coercion versus conversion in Christianity's expansion.
foundation.whyItMatters: Christianity matters as the religion that shaped Western civilization's art, law, philosophy, and political thought, and as the religion whose spread through colonialism transformed the Americas, Africa, and Asia. Understanding Christianity is essential for understanding European history, the history of colonialism, contemporary global politics, and the ongoing debates about religion's role in public life.
questions: Christianity spread first through the Roman Empire's urban networks — along trade routes, through port cities, in the households of merchants and soldiers. What does this tell us about how new religious movements spread? Is the social infrastructure of transmission more important than the content of the message?

The Constantinian establishment — Christianity becoming Rome's official religion — changed the movement in fundamental ways. What was gained and what was lost when a persecuted sect became a state religion? Is there a sense in which Christianity was corrupted by political success?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The key scholarly works span biblical studies (Ehrman, Meier, Crossan on the historical Jesus), patristics (Brown on late antiquity and early Christianity), church history (MacCulloch's comprehensive synthesis), and sociology of religion (Rodney Stark on Christianity's growth). The debate between 'conflict' and 'complexity' accounts of Christianity and science (Lindberg and Numbers) is important for understanding the Galileo affair and its legacy. Feminist and post-colonial scholarship has substantially revised the standard narrative by recovering marginalized voices.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence for early Christianity is primarily textual — the New Testament, the apostolic fathers, the church fathers. Archaeological evidence supplements but rarely overturns textual evidence. The limits: the earliest Christian texts were written decades after the events they describe, by people with theological commitments that shaped what they recorded. The quest for the historical Jesus is a quest to recover a figure through sources that are theological documents, not historical reports.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about Christianity must specify which period, tradition, and question it is addressing. Claims about 'Christianity' as a unified phenomenon across two thousand years and hundreds of traditions are analytically weak. The most productive scholarship focuses on specific transformations: the Pauline reinterpretation of the Jesus movement, the Constantinian establishment, the Gregorian Reform, the Reformation, the global South's emergence as Christianity's center of gravity.
questions: Christianity developed a sophisticated philosophical theology through engagement with Greek philosophy — particularly Platonism and Aristotelianism. Did this theological development clarify Christian belief or distort it? Is the God of the Nicene Creed the same God as the God of the New Testament?

Liberation theology — developed in Latin America in the 1960s-70s — argued that God has a preferential option for the poor and that Christian faith requires political commitment to social justice. The Vatican under John Paul II suppressed liberation theology. What does this conflict reveal about the relationship between Christian theology and political power?

research.topic: The historical Jesus — minimal established facts
research.content: Historical Jesus scholarship has established a minimal core of facts about Jesus of Nazareth with reasonable confidence: he was a Jewish preacher in Roman Palestine, probably active in the late 20s CE; he attracted followers; he was baptized by John the Baptist; he gathered disciples; he was crucified by Roman authorities under Pontius Pilate; his followers believed he had appeared to them after his death. These facts are accepted by the overwhelming majority of scholars, including non-Christian scholars. Everything beyond this core — the content of his teaching, his self-understanding, his miracles — is contested.

Scholarship since the discovery of the Nag Hammadi library (1945) has established that early Christianity was significantly more diverse than the standard narrative suggests. The texts from Nag Hammadi show that Gnostic Christianity was a sophisticated theological tradition with its own scriptures and communities, not a marginal deviation from orthodoxy. Bart Ehrman's synthesis of this scholarship has made it widely accessible. The scholarly consensus now treats 'orthodoxy' as a retrospective category — the winner's version of what Christianity should have been — rather than a description of an original unity from which heresies deviated.

research.topic: The experiences and beliefs of ordinary Christians in the early period
research.content: What ordinary Christians believed, practiced, and experienced in the first three centuries CE cannot be recovered with precision. The surviving texts are almost entirely from educated elite Christians — bishops, theologians, apologists — whose version of Christianity may have differed significantly from the beliefs and practices of ordinary communities. What evidence exists: archaeological material (burial practices, religious objects, graffiti), papyrus documents from Egypt, and references in elite texts to popular beliefs that they found objectionable. What cannot be recovered: the inner religious experience, the specific practices of household churches, and the beliefs of Christians who left no written record.

comparativeNarrative.name: Jewish Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: From within the Jewish tradition, Christianity is a movement that grew out of Judaism, claimed the Jewish scriptures as its own, and then turned against the people it came from. The Church taught for centuries that it had replaced Israel in God's covenant — a doctrine that gave religious backing to centuries of persecution of Jews. This is not a story of two separate religions growing apart in parallel. It is a story of a daughter religion that defined itself partly by rejecting and subordinating its parent. The Holocaust, made possible by long centuries of Christian anti-Semitism, is the catastrophic end point of that relationship.

comparativeNarrative.name: African Christianity Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: The standard history of Christianity runs through Rome and then through Europe, which leaves African Christianity nearly invisible. But Africa was central from the beginning. Alexandria was one of the faith's great intellectual centers. Augustine, whose writings shaped Western Christianity more than almost anyone else, was North African. The Desert Fathers of Egypt created the monastic tradition. The ancient churches of Ethiopia have been Christian since the fourth century. Today the fastest-growing forms of Christianity are in sub-Saharan Africa — Pentecostal, charismatic, deeply rooted in local cultures. Those churches will do more to shape Christianity's future than the older churches of Europe and North America.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: Indigenous peoples subjected to Christian missions
comparativeNarrative.name: Indigenous Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: For indigenous peoples of the Americas, Africa, and Asia, Christian missions arrived with or just after colonial conquest. The connection between gospel and gun was not a coincidence. Missions helped colonial power work: they destroyed indigenous cultures and languages in the name of civilization, they gave moral cover to conquest by casting the conquered as people who needed to be saved, and mission stations served as outposts of colonial administration. This was built into how the missions operated, not a side effect. Today, indigenous Christians grapple with how to hold a faith that was partly forced on their ancestors through violence.

rabbitHole.label: The Roman Empire
rabbitHole.reason: Christianity spread through the Roman Empire's infrastructure and was transformed by its eventual adoption as Rome's official religion — the empire and the religion shaped each other fundamentally.

rabbitHole.label: Islam
rabbitHole.reason: Islam and Christianity share Abrahamic roots, contested claims about Jesus, and a history of conflict and mutual definition — understanding one illuminates the other.

rabbitHole.label: The Protestant Reformation
rabbitHole.reason: The Reformation shattered Western Christianity and produced the religious pluralism that eventually required the development of toleration and secularism as political principles.

rabbitHole.label: Augustine of Hippo
rabbitHole.reason: Augustine's theology — original sin, grace, the relationship between the earthly and heavenly cities — shaped Western Christianity more than any other thinker after Paul.

rabbitHole.label: The Crusades
rabbitHole.reason: The Crusades represent the most violent expression of medieval Christianity's political ambitions and shaped Christian-Muslim relations for centuries.

reference.author: MacCulloch, Diarmaid
reference.title: A History of Christianity: The First Three Thousand Years
reference.annotation: The essential comprehensive history — MacCulloch's 1,100-page synthesis covers Christianity from its origins to the present with remarkable balance and attention to non-Western traditions.

reference.author: Brown, Peter
reference.title: The Rise of Western Christendom
reference.annotation: The essential account of early medieval Christianity — Brown's synthesis shows how Christianity spread through and transformed late antique civilization, with particular attention to the varieties of local Christian practice.

reference.author: Ehrman, Bart
reference.title: Lost Christianities: The Battles for Scripture and the Faiths We Never Knew
reference.annotation: The essential account of early Christian diversity — Ehrman's accessible synthesis of the Nag Hammadi discoveries and the variety of early Christianity provides the essential corrective to the standard narrative of original orthodoxy.

reference.author: Meier, John P.
reference.title: A Marginal Jew: Rethinking the Historical Jesus (4 vols.)
reference.annotation: The most rigorous historical Jesus scholarship — Meier's four-volume work applies the full toolkit of historical-critical method to the New Testament sources and produces the most careful account of what can be known about Jesus.

reference.author: Lane Fox, Robin
reference.title: Pagans and Christians
reference.annotation: The essential account of Christianity's competition with paganism — Lane Fox's history of the first three centuries shows how Christianity triumphed in a religious marketplace and what the triumph cost.

reference.author: MacMullen, Ramsay
reference.title: Christianizing the Roman Empire (A.D. 100–400)
reference.annotation: The essential account of how Christianization actually worked — MacMullen's emphasis on coercion alongside conversion complicates the standard narrative of peaceful voluntary conversion.

commerce.title: A History of Christianity
commerce.author: Diarmaid MacCulloch
commerce.note: The best single starting point — comprehensive, readable, and intellectually honest about the tradition's complexity and failures.

commerce.title: How Jesus Became God
commerce.author: Bart Ehrman
commerce.note: The best accessible account of early Christian theology — Ehrman traces how the understanding of Jesus developed from human prophet to divine figure in the first centuries of the movement.

commerce.title: The Rise of Western Christendom
commerce.author: Peter Brown
commerce.note: Essential for late antiquity and early medieval Christianity — Brown's account shows how Christianity absorbed and transformed Roman civilization.

commerce.title: Lost Christianities
commerce.author: Bart Ehrman
commerce.note: Essential for early diversity — Ehrman's account of the competing Christianities that lost the battle for orthodoxy is the best introduction to the complexity of early Christian history.

commerce.title: A Marginal Jew
commerce.author: John Meier
commerce.note: The most rigorous historical Jesus scholarship — essential for anyone who wants to understand what the historical-critical method can and cannot establish about Jesus.

commerce.title: Pagans and Christians
commerce.author: Robin Lane Fox
commerce.note: The essential account of Christianity's competition with paganism — exhaustive, scholarly, and the best treatment of how Christianity triumphed in the religious marketplace of late antiquity.

popularCulture.title: The Last Temptation of Christ (1988) / The Passion of the Christ (2004)
popularCulture.description: These two films sit at opposite ends of how people picture Jesus. Scorsese shows a human, doubtful Jesus and drew protests from conservative Christians. Gibson's extremely graphic crucifixion drew protests over anti-Jewish imagery. Both caused controversy for the same reason: Christians still fight fiercely over who Jesus was. Neither film fully captures the historical debate, but both show how alive that question still is.

popularCulture.title: Christian holidays in secular culture
popularCulture.description: Christmas and Easter are now secular events in many Western countries where most people no longer go to church. Gift shopping, Easter eggs, and holiday meals carry on even after the religious meaning has faded. This shows how deeply Christianity shaped everyday life. Even when people stop believing, they keep the rhythms, the buildings, and many of the moral ideas that Christianity put in place.

popularCulture.title: Liberation theology and political Christianity
popularCulture.description: In the 1960s and 70s, Latin American theologians including Gustavo Gutiérrez argued that God sides with the poor and that real Christian faith means fighting for justice. This idea shaped the Black church in the US and Catholic social teaching. Pope Francis reflects this tradition, though earlier popes tried to shut it down. It gets the concern for the poor right but leaves out how the Church has also backed powerful rulers.

popularCulture.title: The Young Pope / The New Pope (HBO, 2016-2019)
popularCulture.description: This HBO series follows a fictional American pope and takes seriously the real tensions inside the Catholic Church today: tradition versus reform, authority versus mercy, a shrinking European church versus a growing one in the global South. It gets the internal conflicts right. It distorts by making them more dramatic and personal than they usually are in real church politics.

popularCulture.title: The prosperity gospel as American Christianity
popularCulture.description: The prosperity gospel teaches that faith and giving money to the church will bring you wealth and good health. It treats financial success as a sign of God's approval. It started in the United States and has spread fast through Africa, Latin America, and Asia. It gets the idea of hope right for people with very little. Critics say it exploits the poor by promising things the Bible never actually promised.
